findings.description: Both game blocks include the spurious line `b \getsr \bits` after KeyGen. EUF-CMA and SUF-CMA are non-bit-guessing forgery games — there is no challenge bit `b`, and `b` is never read again. The line should be removed.
sources_consulted: GMR88 abstract (content/References/GMR88 - A Digital Signature Scheme Secure Against Adaptive Chosen-Message Attacks.md)
Katz-Lindell, Introduction to Modern Cryptography 3rd ed., Def. 13.2

findings.description: BLS signatures are described and security is asserted (`EUF-CMA under co-CDH in the random oracle model`) but no reference is given. The standard citation is Boneh, Lynn, Shacham, "Short Signatures from the Weil Pairing" (Asiacrypt 2001 / J. Cryptology 2004).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a [[BLS01 - Short Signatures from the Weil Pairing|BLS01]] citation; create the corresponding reference file.
sources_consulted: Boneh-Shoup, A Graduate Course in Applied Cryptography v0.6, §15.5 (BLS signatures)

findings.description: Page asserts Schnorr signatures are EUF-CMA secure in the ROM citing only Sch91 (which constructs the scheme) and FS86 (which gives the heuristic). The EUF-CMA proof in the ROM is due to Pointcheval-Stern ("Security Proofs for Signature Schemes", Eurocrypt 1996, J. Cryptology 2000) via the forking lemma. Sch91 itself does not contain a ROM EUF-CMA reduction.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a citation to Pointcheval-Stern 1996/2000 alongside Sch91/FS86.

findings.description: The cite `[[LS15 - On the hardness of LWE with binary error|LS15]]` for Dilithium / Module-LWE points to a reference file whose filename is `LS15 - On the hardness of LWE with binary error.md` but whose contents describe Langlois–Stehlé, "Worst-case to average-case reductions for module lattices", DCC 2015 (the actual basis for Module-LWE). The filename and corresponding wikilink-target text are wrong; "On the hardness of LWE with binary error" is a different paper (Buchmann–Göpfert–Player–Wunderer 2016).
findings.suggested_fix: Rename `content/References/LS15 - On the hardness of LWE with binary error.md` → `content/References/LS15 - Worst-case to average-case reductions for module lattices.md` and update both wikilinks (digital-signature.md L169, learning-with-errors.md L127).
sources_consulted: content/References/LS15 - On the hardness of LWE with binary error.md (frontmatter+body describe the Langlois–Stehlé module-lattice paper)

findings.description: The claim "Digital signatures imply OWFs" is asserted without citation. The canonical reference is Rompel 1990 ("One-way functions are necessary and sufficient for secure signatures", STOC 1990), which proves the converse direction (OWF ⇒ DS). The implication DS ⇒ OWF is standard and follows because secure signing requires a one-way mapping from secret randomness to verification key. The argument given ("the signing algorithm is a one-way function") is informal and slightly misstates the OWF candidate.
findings.suggested_fix: Cite Rompel90 and tighten the argument: e.g., "the map (r) ↦ vk where (sk, vk) = KeyGen(r) is a one-way function — inverting it would yield a forgery."
sources_consulted: Goldreich, Foundations of Cryptography Vol. 2, §6.4 (Rompel's theorem)

findings.description: CLAUDE.md requires `\Pr\!\left[...\right]` for probabilities; both advantage definitions and the correctness statement use plain `\Pr[...]`.
Sign is described as outputting `c` in prose ("$\Sign(\sk, m) \to c,$") but the variable is then immediately renamed to `\sigma` ("outputting signature $\sigma \in \calS$"). Inconsistent.

Section heading says "Strong Unforgeability" and game macro is `\sufcma` (rendering `suf-cma`) but the prose label says "**SU-CMA unforgeable**" — inconsistent abbreviation. The standard literature uses sUF-CMA / SUF-CMA.
findings.suggested_fix: Use **SUF-CMA unforgeable** (matching the macro and section heading).

findings.description: Prose ends mid-sentence: "...even given many samples" — missing trailing period and the noun is unclear (samples of what?).
findings.suggested_fix: Reword to: "...even given oracle access to signatures on adversarially-chosen messages."

history.note: 8 findings (1 error, 3 missing_citation, 1 notation, 3 nit, 1 error)

findings.description: The cite `[[LS15 - On the hardness of LWE with binary error|LS15]]` for Module-LWE worst-case-to-average-case hardness points at a reference file whose filename advertises a different paper (Buchmann–Göpfert–Player–Wunderer's binary-error LWE hardness, ~2016). The file body actually describes Langlois–Stehlé, "Worst-case to average-case reductions for module lattices", DCC 75(3):565–599, 2015 — which is the correct attribution. Filename/path is misleading.
findings.suggested_fix: Coordinate with the ds-page fix: rename the reference file and update this wikilink.
sources_consulted: content/References/LS15 - On the hardness of LWE with binary error.md (file body)

findings.description: Page says "Classical reductions (without quantum steps) are known for certain parameter regimes — see subsequent work by Peikert" without an explicit citation. The standard reference is Peikert, "Public-key cryptosystems from the worst-case shortest vector problem" (STOC 2009) and Brakerski–Langlois–Peikert–Regev–Stehlé, "Classical hardness of learning with errors" (STOC 2013).
findings.suggested_fix: Add citations [[Pei09 ...|Pei09]] and [[BLPRS13 - Classical hardness of learning with errors|BLPRS13]].
sources_consulted: Reg05 reference note (notes that classical reductions came in subsequent work)

findings.description: Both security statements write the parameter tuple as `(n,q\chi,m)` — missing comma between `q` and `\chi`. Correct: `(n,q,\chi,m)`. The same tuple appears correctly with all four commas in the game captions and advantage formulas above each statement, so this is clearly a typo.

CLAUDE.md mandates assumption pages follow the order: ## Assumption → ## Known Results → # Variations → # Attacks. This page has: ## Assumption → ## Known Results → # Reduction to lattice problems (top-level, should be ## under Known Results) → ## Cryptographic constructions (orphan ## under a #-level section) → # Attacks → # Variations (Variations and Attacks are reversed; both Attacks and the empty-Attacks placement violate the spec).
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Demote `# Reduction to lattice problems` to `## Reduction to lattice problems` under Known Results. (b) Move `## Cryptographic constructions` somewhere coherent (or promote it). (c) Put `# Variations` before `# Attacks`.
sources_consulted: CLAUDE.md, Page structure for Assumption pages

findings.description: Two definitions on a single `\State` line use a literal `;` separator, which is the documented convention — fine — but `u_0` on the LHS is followed by `\mathbf{u}_1 \getsr \ZZ_q^m` even though the typesetting in the surrounding tuple uses `\mathbf{u}` consistently. The first occurrence on the line drops the bold and writes `\mathbf{u}_0` — actually correct after re-reading. (No fix needed; recording as a passing check.)
`### Search–Decision equivalence` is the only ### subsection inside Known Results and reads slightly odd as a single subhead under ##. Acceptable but worth promoting/folding for readability.

Wikilink `[[Mer78]]` points to a reference file `content/References/Mer78.md` (or any file with alias `Mer78`) that does not exist. The intended source is Ralph Merkle, "Secure communications over insecure channels", CACM 21(4):294–299, 1978.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `content/References/Mer78 - Secure communications over insecure channels.md` (with alias `Mer78`) and update the wikilink to `[[Mer78 - Secure communications over insecure channels|Mer78]]`. Also update the same broken `[[Mer78]]` wikilink in `content/References/BM09 - Merkle Puzzles Are Optimal An O(n2)-Query Attack on Any Key Exchange from a Random Oracle.md`.
sources_consulted: ls of content/References/ (no Mer78 file present)

findings.description: Page states BM09 "tightened the query complexity of the eavesdropper from $O(\ell^6)$ to the optimal $O(\ell^2)$". IR89's bound was a polynomial; the BM09 reference note says "improves on the $\Omega(n^6)$-query lower bound". The IR89 lower bound on the eavesdropper queries was indeed $O(\ell^6)$ in IR89's analysis, so the page is consistent. No change needed; recording as a passing check.

Page heading style mixes title-case (`# Black-Box Separations`, `## Types of Black-Box Reductions`) with sentence-style elsewhere on the site. Other Glossary pages (`# Algebraic group model`, `# Random oracle model`) use sentence case. Internal consistency is not violated, but cross-page consistency is.
findings.suggested_fix: Decide on a sitewide convention; if sentence-case wins, retitle this page accordingly.

findings.description: `(KA)` is rendered in plain text rather than via a macro. The site does not currently define a `\KA` macro, but the rest of the page uses `\PRF`, `\PRG`, `\OT` — using uppercase plain text for one primitive and macros for others is inconsistent.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add a `\KA` macro or wrap occurrences in `\mathsf{KA}` for consistency.
